shortDescription: forEach iterates over an array with a callback and is more readable, but cannot be stopped early. for gives full control including break, continue, and index manipulation.
longDescription: The forEach method is a built-in array function that executes a callback once per element. It is clean and concise for simple traversal but does not support break or continue — the loop always runs to completion. The traditional for loop lets you modify the index, break out early, or skip iterations. for is preferred when you need early termination, performance-critical code, or non-standard index stepping. forEach is preferred for readability when you just need to apply an operation to every element.

shortDescription: All IIFEs are anonymous functions, but not all anonymous functions are IIFEs. An IIFE is an anonymous function that is defined and immediately invoked.
event.target is the element that originally triggered the event (where the user clicked). event.currentTarget is the element the event listener is attached to.

Logical expressions stop evaluating as soon as the result is determined. In &&, if the left side is falsy the right side is never evaluated. In ||, if the left side is truthy the right side is skipped. Useful for defaults and guard clauses.

Both download the script without blocking HTML parsing. async executes the script immediately when downloaded (order not guaranteed). defer executes after the document is fully parsed, in source order.
The period between entering a scope and the point where a let or const variable is declared. Accessing the variable in this window throws a ReferenceError.
Iterates over the values of any iterable (arrays, strings, Maps, Sets, generators). Cannot iterate over plain objects directly.
Iterates over the enumerable property keys of an object, including inherited ones. Avoid using it on arrays.
A function that retains access to variables from its outer (enclosing) scope even after the outer function has finished executing.
Expression returned from an arrow function.
The arguments object (implicit) holds all passed arguments regardless of named parameters. Rest parameters (...args) are the modern explicit alternative.
Functions are treated as values: they can be assigned to variables, passed as arguments, and returned from other functions.
Introduced JSON support, strict mode, and new Array/Object methods (forEach, map, filter, reduce, Object.keys).
Major update introducing Promises, let/const, arrow functions, spread/rest syntax, destructuring, classes, template literals, and ES modules.

When a JS program runs, the engine allocates memory on RAM for variables and objects, and uses a CPU thread to execute instructions. JS is high-level, meaning it abstracts away direct memory and thread management.

Synchronous code runs first. Then microtasks (Promise callbacks, MutationObserver) drain completely. Then macrotasks (setTimeout, setInterval, I/O) run one at a time. After each macrotask, microtasks drain again before rendering.

longDescription: Covers hashbang comments, identifiers (names used to link values), and reserved words that cannot be used as variable names.

shortDescription: A function is a standalone reusable block of code. A method is a function that is a property of an object and operates in the context of that object (via this).

All three set the value of this. call(thisArg, arg1, arg2) invokes immediately with individual args. apply(thisArg, [args]) invokes immediately with an array. bind(thisArg) returns a new function with this permanently bound.
